en: I must restrict myself to this one instance. In Breuer and Freud's book there is a wealth of similar examples. It is easy to understand that scenes such as these make a very strong impression, and accordingly there is an inclination to attribute a causal significance to them in the genesis of the symptoms. The then current conception of hysteria, arising from the English "nervous shock" theory, which Charcot strongly supported, came in conveniently to elucidate Breuer's discovery, hence arose the trauma-theory maintaining that the hysterical symptom and in so far as the symptoms comprise the disease, hysteria itself, arises from some psychic injury (or trauma ), the effect of which is retained in the unconscious indefinitely. Freud, working as Breuer's colleague, amply confirmed this discovery. It was fully demonstrated that not one out of the many hundred hysterical symptoms came down ready made from heaven; they had already been conditioned by past psychic experiences. To some extent, therefore, this new conception opened up a field of very important empirical work. But Freud's tireless spirit of inquiry could not long rest content at this superficial layer, since already there obtruded deeper and more difficult problems. It is obvious enough that moments of great fear and anxiety, such as Breuer's patient went through, would leave behind a lasting effect, but how is it that these happenings are themselves already deeply stamped with the mark of morbidity? Must we suppose that the trying sick-nursing in itself produce such a result? If so, such effects should occur much more frequently, for there are, unfortunately, many trying cases of sick-nursing, and the nurse's nervous constitution is by no means always of the soundest. To this problem medicine gives its admirable answer; the "x" in the calculation is predisposition ; there is a tendency to these things. But for Freud the problem was, what exactly constitutes this predisposition? This question led logically to an investigation of all that had preceded the psychic trauma. It is a matter of common observation that distressing scenes have markedly different effects upon the different participants, and that things which to some are quite indifferent or even pleasant, such as frogs, mice, snakes, cats, excite the greatest aversion in others. There are the cases of women who can calmly be present at a very bad operation, but who tremble all over with horror and nausea at the touch of a cat. By way of illustration let me give the case of a young lady suffering from severe hysteria following a sudden fright. [224] She had been at a social gathering, and was on her way home at midnight accompanied by several acquaintances, when a carriage came up behind them at full speed. All the others moved out of the way, but she, beside herself with fright, ran down the middle of the road just in front of the horses. The coachman cracked his whip and cursed and swore in vain. She ran down the whole length of the street till a bridge was reached. There her strength failed her, and to escape the horses' feet in her despair she would have jumped into the water had not passers-by prevented her. This same lady happened to be in Petrograd during that sanguinary Revolution of the 22nd of January, and saw a street cleared by the volleys of soldiers. All around her people were dropping down dead or wounded, but she retained her calmness and self-possession, and caught sight of a door which gave her escape into another street. These terrible moments agitated her neither at the time nor later on. She was quite well afterwards, indeed felt better than usual.
zh: 我必須將自己限制在這一個案例上。在布羅伊爾與弗洛伊德的著作中，還有大量類似的例子。不難理解，這類場景會留下極深刻的印象，因此人們傾向於認為它們在症狀的產生中具有因果意義。當時對歇斯底里的主流觀念，源自英國的「神經休克」理論——夏科極力支持此說——恰好可用來闡明布羅伊爾的發現，於是創傷理論應運而生。該理論主張：歇斯底里症狀——就症狀構成疾病而言，亦即歇斯底里本身——源於某種心理傷害（或創傷），其影響會無限期地保留在潛意識中。弗洛伊德作為布羅伊爾的同事，充分證實了這項發現。研究完全證明，數百個歇斯底里症狀中，沒有一個是從天而降、憑空出現的；它們無一不受過去心理經驗的制約。因此，在某種程度上，這個新觀念開闢了一個非常重要的經驗研究領域。然而，弗洛伊德那孜孜不倦的探究精神，並不能長久停留在這表層，因為更深層、更困難的問題已經浮現。顯而易見，像布羅伊爾的病人所經歷的那種極度恐懼與焦慮的時刻，會留下持久的影響；但問題是，這些事件本身為何早已深深烙印著病態的痕跡？我們是否必須假定，令人煎熬的看護工作本身就會導致這樣的結果？若是如此，這類效應應該更頻繁地發生才對，因為不幸的是，有許多令人煎熬的看護案例，而看護者的神經體質也絕非總是健全的。針對這個問題，醫學給出了極佳的答案：計算中的「未知數」是素質；存在著一種傾向於此類事物的體質。但對弗洛伊德而言，問題在於：這種素質究竟由什麼構成？這個問題邏輯上引導出對心理創傷之前所有經歷的調查。一個常見的觀察是：令人痛苦的場景對不同的參與者會產生顯著不同的影響；某些事物對一些人來說無關緊要甚至令人愉悅，例如青蛙、老鼠、蛇、貓，卻會引起他人極大的厭惡。有些女性可以冷靜地旁觀一場非常糟糕的手術，但一碰到貓就因恐懼和噁心而渾身顫抖。舉例來說，我提供一位年輕女士的案例，她在一次突然的驚嚇後患上了嚴重的歇斯底里。[^224] 她曾參加一個社交聚會，午夜時分與幾位熟人結伴回家，這時一輛馬車從他們身後全速駛來。其他人都讓開了路，但她卻驚慌失措，徑直跑到馬路中央，正好在馬匹前方。車夫甩響鞭子，咒罵連連，卻無濟於事。她跑過了整條街，直到一座橋前。在那裡，她力氣耗盡，絕望之中為了躲避馬蹄，若非路人攔阻，她早已跳入水中。同一位女士恰好在1月22日那場血腥革命期間身處彼得格勒，目睹士兵的齊射清空了一條街道。她周圍的人紛紛倒地，非死即傷，但她卻保持冷靜與鎮定，並瞥見一扇門，得以逃入另一條街。這些可怕的時刻，無論當時還是事後，都未曾使她激動。她之後身體狀況良好，甚至比平時感覺更好。
